IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—making KB-driven dialogue agents end-to-end differentiable—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that enables jointly training belief trackers and policies with RL and demonstrating improved task success and reward versus Hard-KB/No-KB baselines in simulations and with users. From a methodological perspective this reframes how retrieval can be integrated into neural dialogue agents and will likely change how researchers approach end-to-end KB access. However, practical limitations (strong slot-independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations, and unclear release of code/data) temper its immediate transformative potential, though it opens clear follow-up directions for robustness and generalization. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—making KB lookup differentiable so dialogue agents can be trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning—which is directly relevant to KB-driven conversational systems. Introducing a Soft-KB posterior that integrates with neural belief trackers and an RL policy is likely to change how researchers design trainable info-seeking agents and lowers the barrier to joint optimization of perception and policy. While assumptions (slot independence, static KB, limited handling of new entities) and reliance on simulated/limited movie-KB splits constrain immediate real-world adoption, the approach opens clear follow-up work on richer KB modeling and scalability. Overall, the contribution is likely to be built upon by researchers and practitioners aiming for fully neural, trainable information-access dialogue systems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—making KB-driven dialogue agents end-to-end differentiable—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that enables jointly training belief trackers and policies with RL and demonstrating improved task success and reward versus Hard-KB/No-KB baselines in simulations and with users. From a methodological perspective this reframes how retrieval can be integrated into neural dialogue agents and will likely change how researchers approach end-to-end KB access. However, practical limitations (strong slot-independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations, and unclear release of code/data) temper its immediate transformative potential, though it opens clear follow-up directions for robustness and generalization.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—making KB lookup differentiable so dialogue agents can be trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning—which is directly relevant to KB-driven conversational systems. Introducing a Soft-KB posterior that integrates with neural belief trackers and an RL policy is likely to change how researchers design trainable info-seeking agents and lowers the barrier to joint optimization of perception and policy. While assumptions (slot independence, static KB, limited handling of new entities) and reliance on simulated/limited movie-KB splits constrain immediate real-world adoption, the approach opens clear follow-up work on richer KB modeling and scalability. Overall, the contribution is likely to be built upon by researchers and practitioners aiming for fully neural, trainable information-access dialogue systems.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and communicates its main contributions early (soft-KB lookup and end-to-end trainable KB-InfoBot), with experiments and metrics presented clearly across Movies-KB splits. Notation and methodology are presented consistently (GRU trackers, REINFORCE policy), and figures/tables/examples appear informative given the summary. Minor presentation issues remain: the handling of the artificial KB incompleteness and the independence assumption across slots could be explained more explicitly, and it is unclear whether all implementation details and code are available to fully reproduce results. Overall the writing is clear and adequate, with only modest clarifications needed for full reproducibility. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear flow from problem motivation to the Soft-KB method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and the limitations are explicitly discussed (e.g., independence assumption across slots). Notation and methodology are presented consistently (belief/posterior formulation, GRU trackers, REINFORCE policy) and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective though the summary indicates only ‘‘adequate’’ visual support in places; writing quality is generally clear with minor issues that could be tightened for greater clarity. From a presentation standpoint the paper is solid but could benefit from small edits to improve clarity in some sections (e.g., more explicit linkage between soft retrieval equations and policy inputs).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and communicates its main contributions early (soft-KB lookup and end-to-end trainable KB-InfoBot), with experiments and metrics presented clearly across Movies-KB splits. Notation and methodology are presented consistently (GRU trackers, REINFORCE policy), and figures/tables/examples appear informative given the summary. Minor presentation issues remain: the handling of the artificial KB incompleteness and the independence assumption across slots could be explained more explicitly, and it is unclear whether all implementation details and code are available to fully reproduce results. Overall the writing is clear and adequate, with only modest clarifications needed for full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear flow from problem motivation to the Soft-KB method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and the limitations are explicitly discussed (e.g., independence assumption across slots). Notation and methodology are presented consistently (belief/posterior formulation, GRU trackers, REINFORCE policy) and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective though the summary indicates only ‘‘adequate’’ visual support in places; writing quality is generally clear with minor issues that could be tightened for greater clarity. From a presentation standpoint the paper is solid but could benefit from small edits to improve clarity in some sections (e.g., more explicit linkage between soft retrieval equations and policy inputs).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a meaningful set of baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agent, and an RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker) using the same Movies-KB splits and common success/reward metrics, which supports fair within-experiment comparisons. However, it appears to omit direct empirical comparisons to other recent end-to-end or neural KB-driven dialogue approaches (though related work coverage is described as adequate), and it does not report code release to aid reproducibility. From a methodological standpoint this is a strong evaluation with only minor omissions of contemporaneous competing end-to-end baselines. | B(4): The paper compares against a sensible set of baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agent, and an RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker) using the same Movies-KB splits and consistent success/reward metrics, so comparisons appear fair. Differences between Soft-KB and baselines are analyzed (ablation into Hard vs No-KB) and limitations of the approach are acknowledged. However, while related work coverage is described as adequate, the paper does not clearly demonstrate comparisons to other closely related end-to-end RL KB-access systems from the same era (e.g., other contemporaneous end-to-end RL dialogue efforts), which would strengthen claims of novelty and empirical advantage. Overall the comparisons are strong but miss a few important competing end-to-end baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a meaningful set of baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agent, and an RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker) using the same Movies-KB splits and common success/reward metrics, which supports fair within-experiment comparisons. However, it appears to omit direct empirical comparisons to other recent end-to-end or neural KB-driven dialogue approaches (though related work coverage is described as adequate), and it does not report code release to aid reproducibility. From a methodological standpoint this is a strong evaluation with only minor omissions of contemporaneous competing end-to-end baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a sensible set of baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agent, and an RL agent with hand-crafted belief tracker) using the same Movies-KB splits and consistent success/reward metrics, so comparisons appear fair. Differences between Soft-KB and baselines are analyzed (ablation into Hard vs No-KB) and limitations of the approach are acknowledged. However, while related work coverage is described as adequate, the paper does not clearly demonstrate comparisons to other closely related end-to-end RL KB-access systems from the same era (e.g., other contemporaneous end-to-end RL dialogue efforts), which would strengthen claims of novelty and empirical advantage. Overall the comparisons are strong but miss a few important competing end-to-end baselines.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper makes a concrete technical contribution by introducing a differentiable soft-KB lookup that enables end-to-end training of belief trackers and policies with RL, which is a non-trivial engineering extension beyond classical hard-query or rule-based KB access. However, the high-level problem (RL for dialogue management and information-access dialogues) is well-studied and closely related prior work (including similarly titled papers) already explores end-to-end or RL-based agents, so the overall novelty is moderate rather than groundbreaking. Several methodological pieces (table-structured KB, imitation pretraining then REINFORCE) are incremental refinements rather than entirely new perspectives, so an expert would view this as a solid but incremental advance. | B(3): The paper contributes a concrete technical idea—the Soft-KB lookup as a differentiable posterior over KB entities enabling end-to-end RL training of belief trackers and policies—which is a useful and pragmatic step beyond traditional hard symbolic queries. However, the overall problem (KB-driven dialogue with RL) and many components (neural belief trackers, RNN policies, imitation+REINFORCE training) are well-established in prior literature, and the automated claim analysis indicates only a small set of genuinely novel claims. The authors do distinguish their soft retrieval mechanism from hard/no-KB baselines, but the techniques are best characterized as a non-trivial but incremental extension rather than a paradigm shift that would surprise experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes a concrete technical contribution by introducing a differentiable soft-KB lookup that enables end-to-end training of belief trackers and policies with RL, which is a non-trivial engineering extension beyond classical hard-query or rule-based KB access. However, the high-level problem (RL for dialogue management and information-access dialogues) is well-studied and closely related prior work (including similarly titled papers) already explores end-to-end or RL-based agents, so the overall novelty is moderate rather than groundbreaking. Several methodological pieces (table-structured KB, imitation pretraining then REINFORCE) are incremental refinements rather than entirely new perspectives, so an expert would view this as a solid but incremental advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a concrete technical idea—the Soft-KB lookup as a differentiable posterior over KB entities enabling end-to-end RL training of belief trackers and policies—which is a useful and pragmatic step beyond traditional hard symbolic queries. However, the overall problem (KB-driven dialogue with RL) and many components (neural belief trackers, RNN policies, imitation+REINFORCE training) are well-established in prior literature, and the automated claim analysis indicates only a small set of genuinely novel claims. The authors do distinguish their soft retrieval mechanism from hard/no-KB baselines, but the techniques are best characterized as a non-trivial but incremental extension rather than a paradigm shift that would surprise experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons (Soft-KB vs Hard-KB/No-KB and hand-crafted baselines) across multiple Movies-KB splits, and the method and hyperparameters are described. However, key assumptions and potential confounders are insufficiently controlled: the strong independence assumption across slots is acknowledged but not empirically ablated, the KB incompleteness is artificially constructed, and the summary does not report multiple runs, variance or statistical significance testing. Mathematical components appear sound from the summary, but reproducibility would be strengthened by code release and more rigorous statistical reporting. Overall the evidence supports the conclusions modulo these methodological limitations. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by simulation and user experiments comparing Soft-KB to No-KB, Hard-KB and hand-crafted baselines, showing higher task success and reward. The probabilistic Soft-KB derivation appears coherent and enables end-to-end REINFORCE training; no formal mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. The experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (including ablations), though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the strong independence assumption across slots is a logical limitation that could affect real-world generalization. Overall the conclusions are reasonable given the presented evidence, but the independence assumption and lack of clarity about statistical analyses are notable caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons (Soft-KB vs Hard-KB/No-KB and hand-crafted baselines) across multiple Movies-KB splits, and the method and hyperparameters are described. However, key assumptions and potential confounders are insufficiently controlled: the strong independence assumption across slots is acknowledged but not empirically ablated, the KB incompleteness is artificially constructed, and the summary does not report multiple runs, variance or statistical significance testing. Mathematical components appear sound from the summary, but reproducibility would be strengthened by code release and more rigorous statistical reporting. Overall the evidence supports the conclusions modulo these methodological limitations.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by simulation and user experiments comparing Soft-KB to No-KB, Hard-KB and hand-crafted baselines, showing higher task success and reward. The probabilistic Soft-KB derivation appears coherent and enables end-to-end REINFORCE training; no formal mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. The experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (including ablations), though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the strong independence assumption across slots is a logical limitation that could affect real-world generalization. Overall the conclusions are reasonable given the presented evidence, but the independence assumption and lack of clarity about statistical analyses are notable caveats.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—end-to-end neural dialogue agents with differentiable KB access—and introduces a novel Soft-KB lookup enabling RL training, which is directly relevant to top-tier conference audiences. The methodology (neural belief tracker + RNN policy trained with REINFORCE and imitation learning), the use of multiple Movies-KB splits, and standard task success/reward metrics match the evaluation expectations of the venue. While the independence assumption and limitations around unseen entities are noted, these are acknowledged failures of scope rather than misalignment with the conference; the work is well positioned for the dialogue and RL communities at a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles a core problem in task-oriented dialogue (KB access) with a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup enabling end-to-end RL, which is of clear interest to both communities. The methodology and evaluations (GRU belief trackers, RNN policy trained with REINFORCE, comparisons against sensible baselines across multiple Movies-KB splits and user/simulator evaluations) meet the standards expected at these venues. The authors also acknowledge important limitations (independence assumption across slots, handling unseen entities) and empirically demonstrate improved task success and reward, making it well positioned for the intended audience. Overall the paper is highly relevant and appropriately framed for a top NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—end-to-end neural dialogue agents with differentiable KB access—and introduces a novel Soft-KB lookup enabling RL training, which is directly relevant to top-tier conference audiences. The methodology (neural belief tracker + RNN policy trained with REINFORCE and imitation learning), the use of multiple Movies-KB splits, and standard task success/reward metrics match the evaluation expectations of the venue. While the independence assumption and limitations around unseen entities are noted, these are acknowledged failures of scope rather than misalignment with the conference; the work is well positioned for the dialogue and RL communities at a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles a core problem in task-oriented dialogue (KB access) with a novel differentiable Soft-KB lookup enabling end-to-end RL, which is of clear interest to both communities. The methodology and evaluations (GRU belief trackers, RNN policy trained with REINFORCE, comparisons against sensible baselines across multiple Movies-KB splits and user/simulator evaluations) meet the standards expected at these venues. The authors also acknowledge important limitations (independence assumption across slots, handling unseen entities) and empirically demonstrate improved task success and reward, making it well positioned for the intended audience. Overall the paper is highly relevant and appropriately framed for a top NLP/ML conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a credible experimental evaluation: multiple dataset scales (Small→X-Large), comparisons to reasonable baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agents), and both simulated and real-user tests that support the Soft-KB + end-to-end RL claims. However, key methodological details that I prioritize are thin or missing in the summary: I see no mention of run counts, seed control, variance/confidence reporting, or statistical significance tests, and ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., on the independence assumption, KB sparsity, or policy/belief architecture choices) appear limited. Related work coverage is adequate and limitations are acknowledged (independence assumption, handling new entities), but reproducibility is imperfect despite reported hyperparameters because code/dataset splits and exact experimental protocols are not confirmed. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing in scope but lack the rigorous statistical and ablation detail I would expect for full reproducibility and attribution of effects. | B(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: multiple KB sizes (Small→X-Large), a set of sensible baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, handcrafted agents), and both simulated and real-user evaluations that support the claim that Soft-KB + RL improves success and reward. The paper includes direct comparisons that act as ablations between Soft-KB, Hard-KB and No-KB, but sensitivity analyses for key modeling choices (e.g., the independence-across-slots assumption, robustness to evolving/new entities, and scaling to very large or richer KB schemas) are limited. Related work coverage is adequate and limitations are explicitly discussed (strong independence assumption, inability to handle new entities), though code is not mentioned and some implementation details that affect reproducibility (e.g., full training recipes or data preprocessing for real-user experiments) may be missing. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid but falls short of exhaustive robustness and scalability analyses for real-world deployment.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a credible experimental evaluation: multiple dataset scales (Small→X-Large), comparisons to reasonable baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, hand-crafted agents), and both simulated and real-user tests that support the Soft-KB + end-to-end RL claims. However, key methodological details that I prioritize are thin or missing in the summary: I see no mention of run counts, seed control, variance/confidence reporting, or statistical significance tests, and ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., on the independence assumption, KB sparsity, or policy/belief architecture choices) appear limited. Related work coverage is adequate and limitations are acknowledged (independence assumption, handling new entities), but reproducibility is imperfect despite reported hyperparameters because code/dataset splits and exact experimental protocols are not confirmed. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing in scope but lack the rigorous statistical and ablation detail I would expect for full reproducibility and attribution of effects.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: multiple KB sizes (Small→X-Large), a set of sensible baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, handcrafted agents), and both simulated and real-user evaluations that support the claim that Soft-KB + RL improves success and reward. The paper includes direct comparisons that act as ablations between Soft-KB, Hard-KB and No-KB, but sensitivity analyses for key modeling choices (e.g., the independence-across-slots assumption, robustness to evolving/new entities, and scaling to very large or richer KB schemas) are limited. Related work coverage is adequate and limitations are explicitly discussed (strong independence assumption, inability to handle new entities), though code is not mentioned and some implementation details that affect reproducibility (e.g., full training recipes or data preprocessing for real-user experiments) may be missing. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid but falls short of exhaustive robustness and scalability analyses for real-world deployment.

main_claims: Introduce a Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic (hard) KB queries with a differentiable posterior over KB entities.
Soft-KB lookup enables end-to-end training of neural dialogue agents (belief tracker + policy) with reinforcement learning and yields better dialogue policies than Hard-KB or No-KB variants.
Integrating the soft retrieval with RL leads to higher task success rate and reward in simulations and with real users.
Present a fully neural end-to-end KB-InfoBot (neural belief tracker + neural policy) trainable from user feedback.
_summary.methodology: Construct a soft posterior over KB entities from per-slot belief distributions and a binary known-value probability; use GRU-based neural belief trackers and an RNN policy trained with REINFORCE (and imitation learning initialization) to select dialogue actions and sample results from the KB posterior.
datasets: Movies-KB (four splits: Small, Medium, Large, X-Large)
baselines: Hard-KB lookup (retrieve entities and use entropies)
Hand-crafted rule agent (hand-crafted belief tracker + hand-crafted policy)
RL agent (hand-crafted belief tracker + neural policy)
limitations: Assumes column (slot) values are independent across slots (stated as a strong assumption).
Does not handle new entities or relations at test time.
KB copy used by agent is artificially made incomplete (20% values removed) and user may still know missing values.
Authors note LU / belief-tracker architecture can be a bottleneck and plan to improve it / pretrain on separate data.
_summary.topic_area: Dialogue systems - information access / KB-driven dialogue with reinforcement learning

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with multiple years building and evaluating neural dialogue agents and applied RL systems; experienced in reproducing results from papers and deploying models in production-like settings.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols: seed control, number of runs, and variance reporting
looks for strong baselines and ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component
evaluates statistical rigor: significance testing, confidence intervals, and robustness checks
values reproducibility: expects hyperparameter lists, dataset splits, and code/data release
pays attention to implementation details that affect comparisons (e.g., model sizes, training budgets, pretraining)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the empirical validity and reproducibility of the claims, demanding concrete experiments and clear reporting to justify conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Dialogue Systems Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in dialogue systems and conversational AI with a track record of work on KB-driven dialogue, user modelling, and evaluation of task-oriented agents; follows both academic progress and real-world deployment needs.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work and whether the paper fills an important gap in the literature
evaluates practical impact: scalability to realistic KBs, user experience, and deployment considerations
notices how convincingly the paper links technical contributions to improved end-user outcomes (simulators vs. real users)
values clear conceptual framing and discussion of limitations, generalization, and related work
attends to ethical and UX aspects such as reliability, failure modes, and quality of user evaluation
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's significance and placement in the field, weighing novelty, practical relevance, and clarity of exposition to judge broader impact.
